5:
### Robidoux Hill Historic District 
---  
 #### Date Listed: 1989 
---  
A national historic district that encompasses 61 contributing buildings in a predominantly residential section of St. Joseph. It developed between about 1865 and 1909, and includes representative examples of Italianate, Second Empire, and Queen Anne style architecture. 

Located in the district is the separately listed Edmond Jacques Eckel House designed by architect Edmond Jacques Eckel (1845–1934). Other notable buildings include the Lemon House (1871), Donovan House (c. 1865, 1895), McKinney House (1887), Inslee House (c. 1867), Jonathan M. Bassett (c. 1860, 1880s), and U.S. Weather Bureau Building (1909).
19: ### Hall Street Historic District (1979) --- A nationally recognized local historic area listed on the National Register of Historic Places since 1979. The neighborhood, sometimes called *Millionaire's Row*, was developed between 1870 and 1920 and reflects St. Joseph’s peak prosperity during the westward expansion era, when the city had the highest per-capita income in the U.S. The district spans 19.4 acres (7.9 ha), and initially featured 43 contributing buildings that provided exceptional examples of Gilded Age architecture. It originally included Italianate, Second Empire, Chateauesque, Richardsonian Romanesque, and Queen Anne style homes. Although several structures have been lost to fire and neglect, many of the beautiful surviving homes have been preserved or restored and now serve as bed-and-breakfasts, apartments, or private residences. 18:
### Cathedral Hill Historic District 
---  
 #### Date Listed: 2000 
---  
A national historic district that encompasses 309 contributing buildings, one contributing site, and contributing structures in a predominantly residential section of St. Joseph. It developed between about 1860 and 1950, and includes representative examples of Greek Revival, Italianate, Queen Anne, Colonial Revival, and American Craftsman style architecture. 

Located in the district is the separately listed Virginia Flats apartments. Other notable buildings include the Nisen Stone House (c. 1885), Thomas Culligan House (c. 1872), A. D. Hudnutt House (1909), St. Joseph Cathedral (1877), James Wall House (c. 1880), Taylor Apartments (c. 1860), E. F. Weitheimer House (1888), Sarah and Ann Walsh Apartment House (1915), Henry Owen Stable (1898), George T. Hoagland Speculative House (1901), and James Hull House (1887).
